name: Polars
summary: Rust-native DataFrame library with a lazy execution engine. Drop-in for pandas on large files where you need multithreaded columnar processing without spinning up a cluster.

tasks.task: Load tabular data with lazy evaluation for large files
tasks.mental_trigger: My file is too big for pandas or I want deferred, optimized I/O before filtering
important_params: source — file path or glob pattern e.g. 'data-*.csv'
infer_schema_length — rows to scan for type inference; set to None to scan all rows for mixed-type safety
schema — explicit schema dict to skip inference entirely
tasks.example:
# Lazy: only reads what the query needs
lazy = pl.scan_csv('data.csv', infer_schema_length=None)
result = lazy.filter(pl.col('score') > 0.8).collect()

# Eager for small files
df = pl.read_parquet('data.parquet')
tasks.use_when: Files larger than available RAM. Multi-file glob reads. When you know you will filter aggressively before materializing.
tasks.avoid_when: File fits in memory and you need pandas-compatible API for downstream libraries — polars Series are not pandas Series.
tasks.decision_notes: Always prefer scan_* over read_* for files over ~500MB. The query optimizer pushes filters and column selections down to the I/O layer, often reading less than 10% of the file.
gotchas: pl.read_csv infers types from the first 100 rows by default; mixed-type columns later in the file raise a SchemaError. Pass infer_schema_length=None to scan all rows.
Lazy frames do not execute until .collect() — intermediate results cannot be printed or inspected without materializing.

tasks.task: Select columns and filter rows with expressions
tasks.mental_trigger: I need to select a subset of columns or filter rows using a condition in Polars
important_params: pl.col('name') — column reference inside any expression
alias('new_name') — rename the result of an expression
pl.lit(value) — wrap a scalar as a Polars expression
tasks.use_when: Any time you need to filter rows, add derived columns, or reduce column count in a Polars DataFrame. Chain operations for optimizer efficiency.
tasks.avoid_when: Mixing with pandas ops in the same pipeline — convert once at the boundary using df.to_pandas() or pd.from_polars(df).
tasks.decision_notes: Polars DataFrames are immutable — every operation returns a new DataFrame. This prevents accidental mutation but means you must always reassign the result.
gotchas: Filter expressions must use pl.col() references — passing a Python boolean or a pandas mask raises a TypeError.
with_columns() does not modify in place; always reassign: df = df.with_columns(...).

tasks.mental_trigger: I need per-group statistics: sum, mean, count across one or more key columns
important_params: by — column name or list of column names
maintain_order=True — preserve input row order in group results
exprs — list of aggregation expressions per column
tasks.use_when: Computing per-class statistics, feature aggregation per entity, rolling window summaries. Faster than pandas groupby on large datasets.
tasks.avoid_when: You need a deterministic group row order without setting maintain_order=True — Polars group_by output order is undefined by default.
tasks.decision_notes: Polars group_by is multithreaded and does not require sort-before-group like some SQL engines. For nullable columns, add .fill_null(0) inside the aggregation expression for deterministic results.
gotchas: group_by does not preserve row order; set maintain_order=True if downstream code depends on group sequence.
Aggregating nullable columns without fill_null produces null aggregates that silently propagate through subsequent computations.

tasks.task: Build and execute a lazy query pipeline
tasks.mental_trigger: I want to chain multiple operations and let Polars optimize the execution plan before reading any data
tasks.syntax:
pl.scan_csv(source)
.filter(expr)
.select(cols)
.collect()

lazy_df.fetch(n_rows)  # test on small subset
important_params: collect() — materializes the lazy plan into an eager DataFrame
fetch(n) — execute plan on first n rows only; useful for testing
streaming=True in collect() — for datasets exceeding RAM
tasks.example:
# Build the full plan, execute once
result = (
    pl.scan_parquet('big/*.parquet')
    .filter(pl.col('label') == 1)
    .select(['text', 'label', 'score'])
    .with_columns(pl.col('score').log().alias('log_score'))
    .collect(streaming=True)    # out-of-core for huge files
)

# Test on 100 rows before collecting millions
preview = pl.scan_csv('big.csv').filter(pl.col('x') > 0).fetch(100)
tasks.use_when: Any multi-step transformation on large files where you want the optimizer to eliminate unnecessary column reads and row scans automatically.
tasks.avoid_when: You need to inspect intermediate results at each step — lazy frames cannot be printed without collect() which defeats lazy evaluation.
tasks.decision_notes: fetch(n) is the correct way to test a lazy pipeline without materializing everything. Use collect(streaming=True) for files that exceed RAM — it processes in fixed-size batches.
gotchas: Operations are not validated until collect() is called — a typo in a column name raises an error only at execution time, not at chain time.
df.lazy() on an already-eager DataFrame is valid but adds unnecessary overhead; start from scan_* directly for large files.
